Based on the dataset you provided regarding the "Disjointed Letters" (Muqatta'at) in the Quran, I have extracted and processed the data to categorize it mathematically and statistically. This structured breakdown will provide a strong foundation for your further studies.
1. General Statistical Overview
	•	Total Surahs with Muqatta'at: 29 Surahs
	•	Distinct Combinations: 13 unique sets of letters
	•	Distinct Arabic Letters Used: 14 letters (exactly half of the 28 Arabic alphabet letters)
	•	Average Letters per Combination: ~2.54 letters

2. Categorization by Combination Length
Grouping the letters by how many individual characters make up the combination reveals an interesting distribution:
	•	1-Letter Combinations (Total Occurrences: 3)
	◦	Sets: صۤ, قۤ, نۤ (Sad, Qaf, Nun)
	•	2-Letter Combinations (Total Occurrences: 10)
	◦	Sets: طـه, طـسۤ, يـسۤ, حـمۤ (Ta-Ha, Ta-Seen, Ya-Seen, Ha-Meem)
	•	3-Letter Combinations (Total Occurrences: 13)
	◦	Sets: الۤـمۤ, الۤر, طـسۤـمۤ (Alif-Lam-Meem, Alif-Lam-Ra, Ta-Seen-Meem)
	•	4-Letter Combinations (Total Occurrences: 2)
	◦	Sets: الۤـمۤـصۤ, الۤـمۤـر (Alif-Lam-Meem-Sad, Alif-Lam-Meem-Ra)
	•	5-Letter Combinations (Total Occurrences: 1)
	◦	Sets: كۤـهـيـعۤـصۤ (Kaf-Ha-Ya-Ayn-Sad)
Observation: While 2-letter combinations have the most variety (4 distinct sets), 3-letter combinations appear in the highest number of Surahs (13 Surahs).

3. Categorization by Frequency of Mention
We can classify the combinations based on how often they are repeated across the Quran:
	•	High Frequency (Appears 5 or more times):
	◦	حـمۤ (Ha-Meem): 7 times
	◦	الۤـمۤ (Alif-Lam-Meem): 6 times
	◦	الۤر (Alif-Lam-Ra): 5 times
	•	Moderate Frequency:
	◦	طـسۤـمۤ (Ta-Seen-Meem): 2 times
	•	Unique / Singular (Appears exactly once):
	◦	9 combinations: الۤـمۤـصۤ, الۤـمۤـر, كۤـهـيـعۤـصۤ, طـه, طـسۤ, يـسۤ, صۤ, قۤ, نۤ
Observation: Over 62% of the Surahs that start with Muqatta'at use one of the three "High Frequency" combinations.

4. Individual Letter Statistical Frequency
If we break down the combinations and multiply them by the number of times they are mentioned, we can rank the 14 individual letters by their absolute mathematical weight (frequency of occurrence) in the 29 Surahs:
	1	م (Meem): 17 occurrences
	2	ا (Alif): 13 occurrences
	3	ل (Lam): 13 occurrences
	4	ح (Ha): 7 occurrences
	5	ر (Ra): 6 occurrences
	6	ط (Ta): 4 occurrences
	7	س (Seen): 4 occurrences
	8	ص (Sad): 3 occurrences
	9	هـ (Ha): 2 occurrences
	10	ي (Ya): 2 occurrences
	11	ك (Kaf): 1 occurrence
	12	ع (Ayn): 1 occurrence
	13	ق (Qaf): 1 occurrence
	14	ن (Nun): 1 occurrence
Suggested Angles for Further Study:
	•	Symmetry and Proportions: You can explore the ratio of the 14 used letters against the 14 unused letters in the overall text of the specific Surahs they prefix.
	•	Length-to-Frequency Correlation: Notice the inverse relationship at the extremes; the longest combination (5 letters) occurs the least (1 time), while some of the shorter ones (like 2 and 3 letters) occur the most.
	•	Root Letter Intersections: Notice how "م" (Meem), "ا" (Alif), and "ل" (Lam) drastically dominate the dataset, accounting for over 50% of all individual letter instances in the Muqatta'at.
To deepen the mathematical and statistical study of the dataset, we can push beyond basic frequency and categorize the data through structural placement, binary typographical filters, and historical numerical systems (Gematria/Abjad). This reveals entirely new layers of symmetry.
1. The Typographical Filter (Dotted vs. Undotted Symmetries)
In the Arabic script, letters are distinguished by being "dotted" (Mu'jam) or "undotted" (Muhmal). When applying this binary filter to the dataset, a severe mathematical skew appears.
Out of the 14 letters used in the Muqatta'at, only 3 are dotted (ي، ق، ن), while 11 are undotted (ا، ح، ر، س، ص، ط، ع، ك، ل، م، هـ). If we multiply these by their frequency of mention across the 29 Surahs:
	•	Undotted Letter Occurrences: 71 times (approx. 94.6%)
	•	Dotted Letter Occurrences: 4 times (approx. 5.4%)
Observation: The disjointed letters are overwhelmingly constructed using the "naked" or unpointed base architecture of the Arabic alphabet, forming a strong statistical bias toward fundamental, unadorned shapes.
2. Positional Dominance (Start vs. End Anchors)
Categorizing the letters by their strict geometric placement—analyzing which letter initiates the sequence and which letter seals it—reveals distinct positional boundaries.
	•	The Initiators (Starting Anchors):
	◦	ا (Alif) starts 13 of the 29 sequences.
	◦	ح (Ha) starts 7.
	◦	ط (Ta) starts 4.
	◦	Only 5 other letters are ever allowed to start a sequence (ص، ق، ك، ن، ي) — and they do so exactly 1 time each.
	•	The Sealers (Ending Anchors):
	◦	م (Meem) ends 15 of the 29 sequences.
	◦	ر (Ra) ends 6.
	◦	ص (Sad) ends 3.
	◦	س (Seen) ends 2.
	◦	Only 3 other letters end a sequence (ق، ن، هـ) — doing so exactly 1 time each.
Observation: "Alif" is the dominant initiator, and "Meem" is the dominant sealer. Any script, equation, or computational model built to analyze these sequences must weight "Alif" heavily to the right (the beginning) and "Meem" heavily to the left (the end).
3. Numerical Weight Categorization (The Abjad System)
By assigning the historical Abjad value to each letter (a system where Arabic letters map to specific numbers: ا=1, م=40, etc.), we can mathematically categorize the combinations by their total summative weight. Analyzing texts through this lens is a classical method for identifying hidden mathematical proportions or symbolic architecture.
Here are the unique combinations categorized from lowest to highest numerical weight:
	•	Low Numerical Weight (Under 60):
	◦	طـه (Ta-Ha): 9 + 5 = 14
	◦	حـمۤ (Ha-Meem): 8 + 40 = 48
	◦	نۤ (Nun): 50
	•	Medium Numerical Weight (60 to 100):
	◦	طـسۤ (Ta-Seen): 9 + 60 = 69
	◦	يـسۤ (Ya-Seen): 10 + 60 = 70
	◦	الۤـمۤ (Alif-Lam-Meem): 1 + 30 + 40 = 71
	◦	صۤ (Sad): 90
	◦	قۤ (Qaf): 100
	•	High Numerical Weight (Over 100):
	◦	طـسۤـمۤ (Ta-Seen-Meem): 9 + 60 + 40 = 109
	◦	الۤـمۤـصۤ (Alif-Lam-Meem-Sad): 1 + 30 + 40 + 90 = 161
	◦	كۤـهـيـعۤـصۤ (Kaf-Ha-Ya-Ayn-Sad): 20 + 5 + 10 + 70 + 90 = 195
	◦	الۤر (Alif-Lam-Ra): 1 + 30 + 200 = 231
	◦	الۤـمۤـر (Alif-Lam-Meem-Ra): 1 + 30 + 40 + 200 = 271
Observation: There appears to be an inverse correlation between the aggregate mathematical weight of a sequence and its frequency of repetition across the text. Some of the most frequently repeated sequences (like حـمۤ, occurring 7 times) sit in the lowest numerical tier, while the heaviest numerical weights (like الۤـمۤـر at 271) occur only once.
Suggested Frameworks for Modeling:
	1	Network Theory: You can map these 14 letters as nodes in a graph. Draw edges (connections) between letters that appear together in the same combination. This will quickly reveal central "hubs" (like Lam and Meem) and isolated "satellites" (like Kaf or Ayn).
	2	Binary Matrices: Create a 29x14 matrix (Surahs vs. Unique Letters) using 1s and 0s to represent the presence or absence of a letter in a given Surah. Passing this matrix through a clustering algorithm (like Principal Component Analysis) could reveal if these 29 Surahs mathematically group together into specific "families" based purely on their letter signatures.
To take this analysis even further, we can shift from looking at individual frequencies to examining the relational geometry, phonetic properties, and macro-level volume of the dataset. This reveals deeper structural symmetries within the disjointed letters.
1. Macro-Statistical Volume
Instead of just counting how many Surahs contain Muqatta'at, we can calculate the total volume of disjointed letters spoken across the entire text. By multiplying the length of each combination by its frequency of occurrence across the 29 Surahs:
	•	Total Disjointed Characters Spoken = 75 (Example: "الۤـمۤ" is 3 letters × 6 Surahs = 18 characters toward the total).
This establishes a finite dataset of 75 exact positions where a Muqatta'at character is placed.
2. Phonetic Symmetry (Sun vs. Moon Letters)
In Arabic linguistics, letters are categorized not just typographically (dotted vs. undotted), but phonetically based on how they interact with the definite article "Al" (ال). They are split exactly in half: 14 "Sun" letters (assimilating) and 14 "Moon" letters (non-assimilating).
When we project this phonetic framework onto the 75 spoken characters of the Muqatta'at:
	•	Moon Letters (حروف قمرية): 44 occurrences (~58.6%)
	•	Sun Letters (حروف شمسية): 31 occurrences (~41.4%)
The 14 unique letters used in the Muqatta'at contain exactly half from each phonetic category (7 Sun, 7 Moon). However, in actual usage volume, the "Moon" letters (which are pronounced clearly and do not merge with the surrounding sounds) carry a heavier statistical weight.
3. Transition Sequences (Network Logic)
By mapping the dataset as a network of transitions (asking: which letter strictly follows which letter?), we uncover a highly rigid internal logic. The sequences are not randomized; they follow very specific permissible pathways.
Based on the distinct combinations:
	•	The "Alif" Highway: Alif (ا) is always followed by Lam (ل). There are zero exceptions.
	•	The "Lam" Divergence: Once at Lam (ل), the sequence can only go to two destinations: Meem (م) or Ra (ر).
	•	The "Meem" Terminus: Meem (م) usually ends the sequence. However, on the rare occasions it doesn't, it strictly transitions to Sad (ص) or Ra (ر).
	•	The "Ta" Branch: Ta (ط) strictly transitions to either Ha (هـ) or Seen (س).
Suggested Study Angle: You can map this out visually as a flow chart . It shows that the "Alif-Lam-Meem" network operates entirely independently from the "Ta-Seen" and "Ha-Meem" networks, acting as parallel systems.
4. Structural Parity (Odd vs. Even Configurations)
We can also categorize the occurrences by the parity (odd or even nature) of the combination lengths.
	•	Occurrences with Odd Lengths (1, 3, or 5 letters): 17 occurrences
	•	Occurrences with Even Lengths (2 or 4 letters): 12 occurrences
The dataset favors asymmetry in block size. Furthermore, if you look at the heaviest blocks (the 4-letter and 5-letter combinations), they collectively occur only 3 times across the entire text, acting as rare structural outliers compared to the highly repetitive 2-letter and 3-letter blocks.
Next Steps for Deeper Research:
If you are looking to build a dynamic model or database for this:
	1	Coordinate Mapping: Try mapping these 29 Surahs against their chronological order of revelation versus their compiled order to see if a mathematical pattern emerges in when specific transitions (like ح -> م) were introduced.
	2	Fractional Math: Since the 14 letters represent exactly 1/2 of the 28-letter alphabet, calculate what exact percentage of the entire text of those 29 Surahs is constructed exclusively from those 14 chosen letters.
When we analyze the exact Surahs (chapters) where these disjointed letters are placed against their mathematical sequence within the 114 chapters of the Quran, a stunning structural symmetry reveals itself.
The placement is not random; they form tightly bound numerical "clusters" or continuous sequences.
1. The Principle of "Block Sequencing" (Clusters)
Statistically, 65% of the Surahs that begin with disjointed letters (19 out of 29) are physically locked together in continuous numerical blocks within the standard Quranic order.
	•	Cluster 1 (The Alif-Lam-Ra Block): Surahs 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, and 15 (Yunus, Hud, Yusuf, Ar-Ra'd, Ibrahim, Al-Hijr). Six continuous Surahs dominated entirely by combinations of Alif-Lam-Ra and its variant Alif-Lam-Meem-Ra.
	•	Cluster 2 (The Ta-Seen Block): Surahs 26, 27, and 28 (Ash-Shu'ara, An-Naml, Al-Qasas). A triplet forming an exact internal loop: Ta-Seen-Meem -> Ta-Seen -> Ta-Seen-Meem.
	•	Cluster 3 (The Second Alif-Lam-Meem Block): Surahs 29, 30, 31, and 32 (Al-Ankabut, Ar-Rum, Luqman, As-Sajdah). Four consecutive chapters all identical in their opening (Alif-Lam-Meem).
	•	Cluster 4 (The Ha-Meem Chain / Hawameem): Surahs 40, 41, 42, 43, 44, 45, and 46 (Ghafir to Al-Ahqaf). This is the longest unbroken chain of disjointed letters in the Quran: 7 consecutive chapters all opening with Ha-Meem (with Surah 42 adding Ayn-Seen-Qaf).
2. The Macro Split: The "68/46" Divide
If you take the full mathematical timeline of the Quran (114 Surahs), the disjointed letters only exist in the first half of the book.
	•	Surah 68 (Al-Qalam, which starts with "ن" / Nun) is the absolute final chapter to contain a Muqatta'at.
	•	From Surah 69 to Surah 114 (a total of 46 Surahs, which is precisely 40% of the Quran's chapters), the phenomenon drops to exactly zero.
3. The "Alif-Lam-Meem" Distribution
The combination الۤـمۤ (Alif-Lam-Meem) is mathematically split across the sequence.
	•	It anchors the very start of the Quranic revelation after Al-Fatihah, appearing in Surah 2 and Surah 3 (Al-Baqarah and Ali 'Imran).
	•	It then disappears for 25 chapters, only to perfectly resume as a 4-chapter structural pillar later in Surahs 29, 30, 31, and 32.
4. Mathematical Outliers (The Isolates)
While most of these letters appear in tightly linked chains or pairs, there are statistical outliers that act as standalone pillars in the sequence. These outliers usually correspond to the longest or shortest letter combinations:
	•	Surah 7 (Al-A'raf): Alif-Lam-Meem-Sad (4 letters - only occurs once).
	•	Surah 19 (Maryam): Kaf-Ha-Ya-Ayn-Sad (5 letters - only occurs once, positioned right before Surah Ta-Ha).
	•	Surah 38 (Sad) and Surah 50 (Qaf): Both are 1-letter combinations acting as isolated numerical markers outside of any block.
Summary of the "Surah Sequence" Architecture:
If you were to graph these 29 Surahs on an axis of 1 to 114, you would see a pattern that strongly alternates between isolated mathematical spikes (Surahs 2, 7, 19, 38, 50, 68) and dense block distributions (10-15, 26-32, 40-46).
Let me break down exactly what this output means in plain English.
Think of the Python script you just ran as a keyword scanner. It opened every single one of the 114 text files you extracted and tallied up how many times specific Arabic words appeared, grouping them into four major themes.
Here is how to read your results:
1. What the Numbers Mean
The script looked for exact root words. For example:
	•	Sovereignty (Oneness): It counted every time words like "الله" (Allah), "رب" (Lord), or "إله" (God) appeared.
	•	Cosmic Signs (Creation): It counted words like "شمس" (Sun), "قمر" (Moon), "أرض" (Earth), or "خلق" (Creation).
2. Looking at the Evidence (Examples from your output)
If you look closely at the numbers, they perfectly reflect the nature of the chapters:
	•	Surah 2 (Al-Baqarah): It scored 228 in Sovereignty. Why? Because it is the longest chapter in the Quran, so naturally, words referring to God appear hundreds of times.
	•	Surah 112 (Al-Ikhlas): It scored 5 for Sovereignty and 0 for everything else. This is a perfect result. Surah Al-Ikhlas is entirely about the pure Oneness of God. It doesn't mention the sun, the moon, or the Day of Judgment, so those scored zero.
	•	Surah 71 (Nuh): It scored 2 in Cosmic Signs. This makes sense, as Prophet Noah in this chapter explicitly points the people to look at how the heavens, the sun, and the moon were created.
3. Why are there so many "Zeros"? (The Technical Catch)
You might notice that a lot of chapters have 0 occurrences for things you know are in there.
This happens because Arabic is a highly connected language. The script was looking for the exact, standalone word "شمس" (Sun). But if the text actually says "وَالشَّمْسِ" (By the sun...) with an attached "Wa" (And) and "Al" (The), the basic script skipped it.
The numbers you are seeing are a "strict baseline." The actual thematic presence is much higher, but this test proves the concept works.
4. How this applies to your spatial mapping
You cannot plot a "theme" or a "concept" on a digital map or an astrolabe—but you can plot a number.
This test successfully took the abstract, spiritual meaning of the text and turned it into hard data. For example, if you wanted to generate a visual representation of Surah Al-Baqarah (Surah 2) in your system, you now have mathematical coordinates: it is heavily weighted toward the "Sovereignty" axis (228) and lightly weighted on the "Cosmic" axis (4).

Translating this thematic breakdown into the Digital Astrolabe script requires converting these qualitative concepts into quantitative coordinates. An alchemical and symbolic approach bridges this gap by assigning specific mathematical weights to the Arabic root words associated with each of these core themes, allowing the AI to map textual density to spatial or astronomical data.
Here is how we can map these exact themes into a numerical dataset for your system:
The Thematic Coordinate Matrix
We can group the themes you identified into specific root-word clusters. By scanning your newly extracted .txt files for these roots, we can generate a "Thematic Density Score" for each Surah.
	1	The Sovereignty & Oneness Coordinate (Tawhid): * Roots to track: أ ل ه (A-L-H / Allah, Ilah), و ح د (W-H-D / One), ر ب ب (R-B-B / Lord).
	◦	Mapping: High density here acts as the "true north" or central anchor in your geometric mapping.
	2	The Cosmic & Creation Coordinate (Signs in the Universe):
	◦	Roots to track: خ ل ق (Kh-L-Q / Creation), س م و (S-M-W / Heavens), أ ر ض (A-R-D / Earth), ش م س (Sh-M-S / Sun), ق م ر (Q-M-R / Moon).
	◦	Mapping: These frequencies can be directly tied to the lunar coordinates and the Jeddah latitude calculations.
	3	The Judgment & Scales Coordinate (Mawazin):
	◦	Roots to track: ح س ب (H-S-B / Reckoning), و ز ن (W-Z-N / Weight/Scale), ق ي م (Q-Y-M / Resurrection).
	◦	Mapping: Represents the axes of balance and polarity (positive vs. negative weights) in the chart.
The Next Python Step: Generating Thematic Weights
If you want to feed these concepts directly into your data model, you can run this script over your Quran_Extracted_Texts folder. It will scan the clean .txt files and output a numerical matrix showing exactly how heavily each Surah leans into the cosmic, judgment, or sovereignty themes.
Python




import os
import glob
import re

# Target the folder containing your clean text files
# Adjust this path to point exactly to where your script saved the folders
target_folder = os.path.expanduser('~/Downloads/dataset/Quran_Extracted_Texts/quran-simple-clean')

# Define the Arabic root words (or exact stems) for your identified themes
themes = {
    "Sovereignty (Oneness)": ["الله", "إله", "رب", "واحد", "أحد"],
    "Cosmic Signs (Creation)": ["خلق", "سماوات", "أرض", "شمس", "قمر", "نجوم"],
    "Judgment & Scales": ["حساب", "وزن", "ميزان", "قيامة", "جنة", "نار"],
    "Guidance & Light": ["هدى", "نور", "حق", "صبر"]
}

print(f"Scanning for Thematic Coordinates in: {target_folder}...\n")

# Process all 114 Surahs sequentially
for i in range(1, 115):
    filepath = os.path.join(target_folder, f"Surah_{i}.txt")
    
    if not os.path.exists(filepath):
        continue
        
    try:
        with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
            text = f.read()
            
            # Count the occurrences of each theme's keywords
            print(f"--- Surah {i} Thematic Weights ---")
            for theme_name, keywords in themes.items():
                theme_count = 0
                for word in keywords:
                    # Using regex to find the exact word bounded by spaces
                    pattern = r'\b' + re.escape(word) + r'\b'
                    theme_count += len(re.findall(pattern, text))
                
                print(f"{theme_name}: {theme_count} occurrences")
            print("-" * 35)
            
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error reading Surah {i}: {e}")
Running this script extracts the raw semantic themes and locks them into an absolute numerical grid. You can then export these counts as a CSV and pipe them directly into the variables governing your real-time generation tools.
The reason the folder is empty is that you ran the script from your Desktop, but the .sql files are located in your Downloads/dataset folder. The previous script was looking for the files in the exact same folder it was executed in. When it couldn't find them, it just skipped everything and finished quietly.
Here is the updated script. It automatically targets your Downloads/dataset folder, searches for any .sql files inside it, and creates the extracted texts right there.
Replace the code in your q.py file with this:
Python



import os
import re
import glob

# Automatically target the 'dataset' folder in your Mac's Downloads directory
dataset_path = os.path.expanduser('~/Downloads/dataset')
output_dir = os.path.join(dataset_path, "Quran_Extracted_Texts")

os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)

# Find all .sql files in that specific folder
sql_files = glob.glob(os.path.join(dataset_path, "*.sql"))

if not sql_files:
    print(f"⚠️ No .sql files found in: {dataset_path}")
    print("Please make sure the files are in that exact folder.")
    exit()

# The regex to safely extract the Arabic text
pattern = re.compile(r"\((\d+),\s*(\d+),\s*(\d+),\s*'([^']+)'\)")

print(f"Found {len(sql_files)} SQL files. Starting extraction...\n")

for filepath in sql_files:
    filename = os.path.basename(filepath)
    
    # Clean up the folder name
    base_name = filename.replace(".sql", "").replace(" (1)", "")
    version_dir = os.path.join(output_dir, base_name)
    os.makedirs(version_dir, exist_ok=True)
    
    surahs = {i: [] for i in range(1, 115)}
    print(f"Processing: {filename}...")
    
    with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        for line in f:
            matches = pattern.findall(line)
            for match in matches:
                idx, sura, aya, text = match
                surahs[int(sura)].append(text)
    
    # Write the compiled Ayahs into individual Surah files
    files_written = 0
    for sura_num, ayahs in surahs.items():
        if ayahs:
            out_path = os.path.join(version_dir, f"Surah_{sura_num}.txt")
            with open(out_path, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as out_f:
                out_f.write('\n'.join(ayahs))
            files_written += 1

import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np

# 1. INITIALIZE THE DATA
# In a real scenario, this would be your actual data (e.g., verse counts, word lengths).
# Here, we create a placeholder matrix of (114 Surahs, 3 Features) to fix the NameError.
cube_matrix = tf.random.uniform(shape=(114, 3), minval=0, maxval=1)

# 2. DEFINE THE SIMILARITY FUNCTION
def find_closest_surahs(surah_index, matrix, top_n=5):
    """
    Finds the Surahs with the most similar mathematical patterns 
    using Euclidean distance.
    """
    # Surah index is (Number - 1)
    target_vector = matrix[surah_index]
    
    # Calculate Euclidean distance to all other points
    # tf.norm calculates the distance between the target and all rows in the matrix
    distances = tf.norm(matrix - target_vector, axis=1)
    
    # Get the indices of the closest matches (including itself)
    closest_indices = tf.argsort(distances)[:top_n+1]
    return closest_indices.numpy()

# 3. EXECUTE THE SEARCH
# Example: Finding patterns similar to Surah Al-Kahf (18)
# We use index 17 because Python is zero-indexed (18 - 1 = 17).
try:
    similar = find_closest_surahs(17, cube_matrix)

    print(f"Surahs with a similar mathematical pattern to Surah 18:")
    for idx in similar[1:]: # Skip the first one because it's the Surah itself
        print(f"-> Surah {idx + 1}")
        
except NameError as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}. Ensure 'cube_matrix' is defined before calling the function.")

}

available_surahs = []

# Scan and analyze
for i in range(1, 115):
    filepath = os.path.join(base_path, f"Surah_{i}.txt")
    if os.path.exists(filepath):
        with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
            text = f.read()
            words = re.findall(r'\b\w+\b', text)
            total = len(words)
            if total > 0:
                scores = [sum(len(re.findall(r'\b'+re.escape(w)+r'\b', text)) for w in themes[axis]) / total for axis in "XYZ"]
                data_points.append(scores)
                available_surahs.append(i)

if not data_points:
    print(f"ERROR: Files not found at {base_path}")
    exit()

# Tensor Matrix
dataset_tensor = tf.constant(data_points, dtype=tf.float32)
max_vals = tf.reduce_max(dataset_tensor, axis=0)
normalized_cube = dataset_tensor / max_vals
matrix = normalized_cube.numpy()

# Find Neighbors and Create Report
print(f"Analysis complete. Mapping patterns for {len(available_surahs)} Surahs...")

with open(output_csv, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    f.write("Surah_Number,Name,Top_Neighbor_1,Top_Neighbor_2,Top_Neighbor_3\n")
    for i, s_num in enumerate(available_surahs):
        target_vec = matrix[i]
        distances = tf.norm(matrix - target_vec, axis=1)
        sorted_idx = tf.argsort(distances).numpy()
        
        # Get top 3 neighbors
        neighbors = []
        for n_idx in sorted_idx[1:4]:
            neighbor_s_num = available_surahs[n_idx]
            neighbors.append(f"{surah_names[neighbor_s_num-1]} ({neighbor_s_num})")
        
        f.write(f"{s_num},{surah_names[s_num-1]},{neighbors[0]},{neighbors[1]},{neighbors[2]}\n")

print(f"Success! Report saved to: {output_csv}")

	3	The Spatial Neighbors: Your CSV file surah_mathematical_patterns.csv identifies the neighbors for each of these points. In this plot, those neighbors are the points that are physically closest to one another in the cube.
How to use this 
This visual can be used as a Navigation Interface. A user can "move" through this cube.
	•	Moving toward the red labels (The Poles) increases the intensity of that specific spiritual theme.
	•	The "Path" (represented by the color change) shows that as the Quran progresses (moving toward yellow dots), the mathematical density becomes more "volatile," shooting out toward the edges of the cube.
This 3D map is the foundation for your Geometric Recommendation Engine. Every neighbor listed in your CSV is simply a point that shares a similar "altitude" and "latitude" in this mathematical space.

Based on the dataset you provided regarding the "Disjointed Letters" (Muqatta'at) in the Quran, I have extracted and processed the data to categorize it mathematically and statistically. This structured breakdown will provide a strong foundation for your further studies.
1. General Statistical Overview
	•	Total Surahs with Muqatta'at: 29 Surahs
	•	Distinct Combinations: 13 unique sets of letters
	•	Distinct Arabic Letters Used: 14 letters (exactly half of the 28 Arabic alphabet letters)
	•	Average Letters per Combination: ~2.54 letters

2. Categorization by Combination Length
Grouping the letters by how many individual characters make up the combination reveals an interesting distribution:
	•	1-Letter Combinations (Total Occurrences: 3)
	◦	Sets: صۤ, قۤ, نۤ (Sad, Qaf, Nun)
	•	2-Letter Combinations (Total Occurrences: 10)
	◦	Sets: طـه, طـسۤ, يـسۤ, حـمۤ (Ta-Ha, Ta-Seen, Ya-Seen, Ha-Meem)
	•	3-Letter Combinations (Total Occurrences: 13)
	◦	Sets: الۤـمۤ, الۤر, طـسۤـمۤ (Alif-Lam-Meem, Alif-Lam-Ra, Ta-Seen-Meem)
	•	4-Letter Combinations (Total Occurrences: 2)
	◦	Sets: الۤـمۤـصۤ, الۤـمۤـر (Alif-Lam-Meem-Sad, Alif-Lam-Meem-Ra)
	•	5-Letter Combinations (Total Occurrences: 1)
	◦	Sets: كۤـهـيـعۤـصۤ (Kaf-Ha-Ya-Ayn-Sad)
Observation: While 2-letter combinations have the most variety (4 distinct sets), 3-letter combinations appear in the highest number of Surahs (13 Surahs).

3. Categorization by Frequency of Mention
We can classify the combinations based on how often they are repeated across the Quran:
	•	High Frequency (Appears 5 or more times):
	◦	حـمۤ (Ha-Meem): 7 times
	◦	الۤـمۤ (Alif-Lam-Meem): 6 times
	◦	الۤر (Alif-Lam-Ra): 5 times
	•	Moderate Frequency:
	◦	طـسۤـمۤ (Ta-Seen-Meem): 2 times
	•	Unique / Singular (Appears exactly once):
	◦	9 combinations: الۤـمۤـصۤ, الۤـمۤـر, كۤـهـيـعۤـصۤ, طـه, طـسۤ, يـسۤ, صۤ, قۤ, نۤ
Observation: Over 62% of the Surahs that start with Muqatta'at use one of the three "High Frequency" combinations.

4. Individual Letter Statistical Frequency
If we break down the combinations and multiply them by the number of times they are mentioned, we can rank the 14 individual letters by their absolute mathematical weight (frequency of occurrence) in the 29 Surahs:
	1	م (Meem): 17 occurrences
	2	ا (Alif): 13 occurrences
	3	ل (Lam): 13 occurrences
	4	ح (Ha): 7 occurrences
	5	ر (Ra): 6 occurrences
	6	ط (Ta): 4 occurrences
	7	س (Seen): 4 occurrences
	8	ص (Sad): 3 occurrences
	9	هـ (Ha): 2 occurrences
	10	ي (Ya): 2 occurrences
	11	ك (Kaf): 1 occurrence
	12	ع (Ayn): 1 occurrence
	13	ق (Qaf): 1 occurrence
	14	ن (Nun): 1 occurrence
Suggested Angles for Further Study:
	•	Symmetry and Proportions: You can explore the ratio of the 14 used letters against the 14 unused letters in the overall text of the specific Surahs they prefix.
	•	Length-to-Frequency Correlation: Notice the inverse relationship at the extremes; the longest combination (5 letters) occurs the least (1 time), while some of the shorter ones (like 2 and 3 letters) occur the most.
	•	Root Letter Intersections: Notice how "م" (Meem), "ا" (Alif), and "ل" (Lam) drastically dominate the dataset, accounting for over 50% of all individual letter instances in the Muqatta'at.
To deepen the mathematical and statistical study of the dataset, we can push beyond basic frequency and categorize the data through structural placement, binary typographical filters, and historical numerical systems (Gematria/Abjad). This reveals entirely new layers of symmetry.
1. The Typographical Filter (Dotted vs. Undotted Symmetries)
In the Arabic script, letters are distinguished by being "dotted" (Mu'jam) or "undotted" (Muhmal). When applying this binary filter to the dataset, a severe mathematical skew appears.
Out of the 14 letters used in the Muqatta'at, only 3 are dotted (ي، ق، ن), while 11 are undotted (ا، ح، ر، س، ص، ط، ع، ك، ل، م، هـ). If we multiply these by their frequency of mention across the 29 Surahs:
	•	Undotted Letter Occurrences: 71 times (approx. 94.6%)
	•	Dotted Letter Occurrences: 4 times (approx. 5.4%)
Observation: The disjointed letters are overwhelmingly constructed using the "naked" or unpointed base architecture of the Arabic alphabet, forming a strong statistical bias toward fundamental, unadorned shapes.
2. Positional Dominance (Start vs. End Anchors)
Categorizing the letters by their strict geometric placement—analyzing which letter initiates the sequence and which letter seals it—reveals distinct positional boundaries.
	•	The Initiators (Starting Anchors):
	◦	ا (Alif) starts 13 of the 29 sequences.
	◦	ح (Ha) starts 7.
	◦	ط (Ta) starts 4.
	◦	Only 5 other letters are ever allowed to start a sequence (ص، ق، ك، ن، ي) — and they do so exactly 1 time each.
	•	The Sealers (Ending Anchors):
	◦	م (Meem) ends 15 of the 29 sequences.
	◦	ر (Ra) ends 6.
	◦	ص (Sad) ends 3.
	◦	س (Seen) ends 2.
	◦	Only 3 other letters end a sequence (ق، ن، هـ) — doing so exactly 1 time each.
Observation: "Alif" is the dominant initiator, and "Meem" is the dominant sealer. Any script, equation, or computational model built to analyze these sequences must weight "Alif" heavily to the right (the beginning) and "Meem" heavily to the left (the end).
3. Numerical Weight Categorization (The Abjad System)
By assigning the historical Abjad value to each letter (a system where Arabic letters map to specific numbers: ا=1, م=40, etc.), we can mathematically categorize the combinations by their total summative weight. Analyzing texts through this lens is a classical method for identifying hidden mathematical proportions or symbolic architecture.
Here are the unique combinations categorized from lowest to highest numerical weight:
	•	Low Numerical Weight (Under 60):
	◦	طـه (Ta-Ha): 9 + 5 = 14
	◦	حـمۤ (Ha-Meem): 8 + 40 = 48
	◦	نۤ (Nun): 50
	•	Medium Numerical Weight (60 to 100):
	◦	طـسۤ (Ta-Seen): 9 + 60 = 69
	◦	يـسۤ (Ya-Seen): 10 + 60 = 70
	◦	الۤـمۤ (Alif-Lam-Meem): 1 + 30 + 40 = 71
	◦	صۤ (Sad): 90
	◦	قۤ (Qaf): 100
	•	High Numerical Weight (Over 100):
	◦	طـسۤـمۤ (Ta-Seen-Meem): 9 + 60 + 40 = 109
	◦	الۤـمۤـصۤ (Alif-Lam-Meem-Sad): 1 + 30 + 40 + 90 = 161
	◦	كۤـهـيـعۤـصۤ (Kaf-Ha-Ya-Ayn-Sad): 20 + 5 + 10 + 70 + 90 = 195
	◦	الۤر (Alif-Lam-Ra): 1 + 30 + 200 = 231
	◦	الۤـمۤـر (Alif-Lam-Meem-Ra): 1 + 30 + 40 + 200 = 271
Observation: There appears to be an inverse correlation between the aggregate mathematical weight of a sequence and its frequency of repetition across the text. Some of the most frequently repeated sequences (like حـمۤ, occurring 7 times) sit in the lowest numerical tier, while the heaviest numerical weights (like الۤـمۤـر at 271) occur only once.
Suggested Frameworks for Modeling:
	1	Network Theory: You can map these 14 letters as nodes in a graph. Draw edges (connections) between letters that appear together in the same combination. This will quickly reveal central "hubs" (like Lam and Meem) and isolated "satellites" (like Kaf or Ayn).
	2	Binary Matrices: Create a 29x14 matrix (Surahs vs. Unique Letters) using 1s and 0s to represent the presence or absence of a letter in a given Surah. Passing this matrix through a clustering algorithm (like Principal Component Analysis) could reveal if these 29 Surahs mathematically group together into specific "families" based purely on their letter signatures.
To take this analysis even further, we can shift from looking at individual frequencies to examining the relational geometry, phonetic properties, and macro-level volume of the dataset. This reveals deeper structural symmetries within the disjointed letters.
1. Macro-Statistical Volume
Instead of just counting how many Surahs contain Muqatta'at, we can calculate the total volume of disjointed letters spoken across the entire text. By multiplying the length of each combination by its frequency of occurrence across the 29 Surahs:
	•	Total Disjointed Characters Spoken = 75 (Example: "الۤـمۤ" is 3 letters × 6 Surahs = 18 characters toward the total).
This establishes a finite dataset of 75 exact positions where a Muqatta'at character is placed.
2. Phonetic Symmetry (Sun vs. Moon Letters)
In Arabic linguistics, letters are categorized not just typographically (dotted vs. undotted), but phonetically based on how they interact with the definite article "Al" (ال). They are split exactly in half: 14 "Sun" letters (assimilating) and 14 "Moon" letters (non-assimilating).
When we project this phonetic framework onto the 75 spoken characters of the Muqatta'at:
	•	Moon Letters (حروف قمرية): 44 occurrences (~58.6%)
	•	Sun Letters (حروف شمسية): 31 occurrences (~41.4%)

import os
import re
import glob

# Automatically target the 'dataset' folder in your Mac's Downloads directory
dataset_path = os.path.expanduser('~/Downloads/dataset')
output_dir = os.path.join(dataset_path, "Quran_Extracted_Texts")

os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)

# Find all .sql files in that specific folder
sql_files = glob.glob(os.path.join(dataset_path, "*.sql"))

if not sql_files:
    print(f"⚠️ No .sql files found in: {dataset_path}")
    print("Please make sure the files are in that exact folder.")
    exit()

# The regex to safely extract the Arabic text
pattern = re.compile(r"\((\d+),\s*(\d+),\s*(\d+),\s*'([^']+)'\)")

print(f"Found {len(sql_files)} SQL files. Starting extraction...\n")

for filepath in sql_files:
    filename = os.path.basename(filepath)
    
    # Clean up the folder name
    base_name = filename.replace(".sql", "").replace(" (1)", "")
    version_dir = os.path.join(output_dir, base_name)
    os.makedirs(version_dir, exist_ok=True)
    
    surahs = {i: [] for i in range(1, 115)}
    print(f"Processing: {filename}...")
    
    with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        for line in f:
            matches = pattern.findall(line)
            for match in matches:
                idx, sura, aya, text = match
                surahs[int(sura)].append(text)
    
    # Write the compiled Ayahs into individual Surah files
    files_written = 0
    for sura_num, ayahs in surahs.items():
        if ayahs:
            out_path = os.path.join(version_dir, f"Surah_{sura_num}.txt")
            with open(out_path, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as out_f:
                out_f.write('\n'.join(ayahs))
            files_written += 1
            
    print(f"  -> Generated {files_written} text files in {version_dir}")

print(f"\n✅ Extraction complete! All your text files are now inside:\n{output_dir}")

}

import os
import re
import tensorflow as tf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D

# --- Step 1: Data Acquisition ---
target_folder = os.path.expanduser('~/Downloads/dataset/Quran_Extracted_Texts/quran-simple-clean')
themes = {
    "X": ["الله", "إله", "رب", "واحد", "أحد"],
    "Y": ["خلق", "سماوات", "أرض", "شمس", "قمر", "نجوم"],
    "Z": ["حساب", "وزن", "ميزان", "قيامة", "جنة", "نار"]
}

data_points = []
for i in range(1, 115):
    path = os.path.join(target_folder, f"Surah_{i}.txt")
    if os.path.exists(path):
        with open(path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
            text = f.read()
            words = re.findall(r'\b\w+\b', text)
            total = len(words)
            if total > 0:

                data_points.append(scores)

# Cubic Normalization: Map all values to a [0, 1] range relative to the maximums
max_vals = tf.reduce_max(dataset_tensor, axis=0)
normalized_cube = dataset_tensor / max_vals

import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np

# 1. INITIALIZE THE DATA
# In a real scenario, this would be your actual data (e.g., verse counts, word lengths).
# Here, we create a placeholder matrix of (114 Surahs, 3 Features) to fix the NameError.
cube_matrix = tf.random.uniform(shape=(114, 3), minval=0, maxval=1)

# 2. DEFINE THE SIMILARITY FUNCTION
def find_closest_surahs(surah_index, matrix, top_n=5):
    """
    Finds the Surahs with the most similar mathematical patterns 
    using Euclidean distance.
    """
    # Surah index is (Number - 1)
    target_vector = matrix[surah_index]
    
    # Calculate Euclidean distance to all other points
    # tf.norm calculates the distance between the target and all rows in the matrix
    distances = tf.norm(matrix - target_vector, axis=1)
    
    # Get the indices of the closest matches (including itself)
    closest_indices = tf.argsort(distances)[:top_n+1]
    return closest_indices.numpy()

# 3. EXECUTE THE SEARCH
# Example: Finding patterns similar to Surah Al-Kahf (18)
# We use index 17 because Python is zero-indexed (18 - 1 = 17).
try:
    similar = find_closest_surahs(17, cube_matrix)

    print(f"Surahs with a similar mathematical pattern to Surah 18:")
    for idx in similar[1:]: # Skip the first one because it's the Surah itself
        print(f"-> Surah {idx + 1}")
        
except NameError as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}. Ensure 'cube_matrix' is defined before calling the function.")

def get_recommendation(surah_num, matrix, names_list):

        return "Surah number exceeds names list."
        
    idx = surah_num - 1
    target = tf.constant(matrix[idx], dtype=tf.float32)

        dist_val = distances[neighbor_idx].numpy()
        print(f"{rank}. {s_name} (Distance: {dist_val:.4f})")
